q: What is acute infection-induced encephalopathy?
a: A form of brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) triggered acutely by an infectious illness
d: A chronic brain disease caused by bacterial meningitis
A form of encephalopathy caused by a genetic mutation alone
A brain condition caused exclusively by viral encephalitis
A metabolic disorder causing gradual brain deterioration
A surgical complication causing postoperative brain swelling
A drug-induced toxic encephalopathy
An autoimmune encephalopathy with no infectious trigger
A traumatic brain injury mimicking infection symptoms
e: Acute infection-induced encephalopathy is a severe but sometimes reversible brain syndrome that occurs during systemic infections, especially in children. The exact mechanism may involve cytokine storms, direct viral invasion, or metabolic derangement. It is distinct from direct viral encephalitis where the pathogen invades brain tissue.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to brain_neuro. Distractors replaced with neurological alternatives.

q: What is microcephaly with chorioretinopathy?
a: A genetic condition characterized by abnormally small head size combined with degeneration of the retina and choroid
d: Microcephaly caused solely by intrauterine Zika virus infection
Microcephaly with no associated eye findings
A condition causing abnormally large head size with retinal damage
Chorioretinopathy without any brain abnormality
Microcephaly caused by maternal rubella infection only
A chromosomal trisomy causing brain and eye defects
A metabolic disorder causing progressive brain and retinal decline
Microcephaly caused by birth trauma without retinal involvement
e: Microcephaly with chorioretinopathy is caused by mutations in genes such as TUBGCP6 and TUBGCP4. It is a congenital syndrome where defective tubulin components disrupt both brain development (causing microcephaly) and retinal development (causing chorioretinopathy and vision impairment).
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic syndrome). Distractors improved.

q: What is microcephaly with short stature and impaired glucose metabolism?
a: A rare genetic syndrome combining abnormally small head size, short stature, and defects in glucose regulation
d: A form of microcephaly caused by intrauterine infection without metabolic involvement
A chromosomal trisomy causing microcephaly and obesity
A nutritional syndrome caused by fetal iodine deficiency
A form of dwarfism with normal head size and glucose metabolism
An endocrine disorder with short stature but normal brain size
A mitochondrial disease causing brain and metabolic dysfunction
A Down syndrome variant with short stature
A lysosomal storage disorder causing microcephaly
e: This rare autosomal recessive syndrome is caused by mutations in the TRMT10A gene (tRNA methyltransferase), which disrupts tRNA modification and affects pancreatic beta cell function (causing glucose intolerance) and neuronal development (causing microcephaly and intellectual disability).
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic syndrome). Distractors improved.

q: What is infantile hypercalcemia?
a: A condition of abnormally high blood calcium levels in infants, which can cause failure to thrive, vomiting, and kidney damage
d: Abnormally low blood calcium in infants causing seizures
Normal age-related fluctuation in infant calcium levels
High blood phosphate causing calcification in infants
Excess dietary vitamin D causing bone overgrowth in infants
A condition of high calcium in adults only
Low calcium absorption from the gut in premature infants
High blood magnesium mimicking calcium toxicity
Calcium deficiency causing rickets in infants
e: Infantile hypercalcemia can be caused by mutations in CYP24A1 (impairing vitamin D breakdown) or SLC34A1 (affecting phosphate transport), leading to excessive active vitamin D and calcium absorption. It can also occur in Williams syndrome (chromosome 7 deletion). Treatment includes restricting vitamin D and calcium intake.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (often genetic etiology). Distractors replaced with calcium-disorder alternatives.

q: What is pyruvate kinase hyperactivity?
a: A rare metabolic condition where pyruvate kinase enzyme activity is abnormally elevated, affecting red blood cell energy metabolism
d: A deficiency of pyruvate kinase causing hemolytic anemia
A deficiency of pyruvate kinase causing muscle weakness
Excess pyruvate accumulation from blocked enzyme activity
A condition where pyruvate kinase is completely absent
Normal variation in pyruvate kinase activity between individuals
A type of diabetes caused by impaired pyruvate metabolism
A mitochondrial disease causing lactic acidosis from low pyruvate kinase
A cancer mutation activating pyruvate kinase in tumor cells
e: Pyruvate kinase hyperactivity is caused by gain-of-function mutations in the PKLR gene, leading to overactive red cell pyruvate kinase. Paradoxically, this can cause hemolytic anemia because excessive glycolytic flux depletes ATP and 2,3-BPG, impairing red cell function. It contrasts with the more common pyruvate kinase deficiency.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic enzyme disorder). Distractors replaced.

q: What is dystelephalangy?
a: A congenital deformity characterized by abnormal angulation or curvature of the terminal phalanges (fingertip bones)
d: Absence of all fingers (ectrodactyly)
Extra fingers on the hand (polydactyly)
Fusion of two or more fingers (syndactyly)
Abnormally short metacarpal bones in the hand
Progressive destruction of finger bones from arthritis
Congenital absence of finger nails
Overgrowth of the terminal phalanges
Inward curving of the entire finger (clinodactyly)
e: Dystelephalangy refers to malformation specifically of the distal (terminal) phalanges — the last bone segment of each finger. It can cause the fingertips to appear bent or deformed. It is a feature of several genetic syndromes including Coffin-Siris syndrome and Turner syndrome.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (congenital genetic feature). Distractors replaced with finger deformity alternatives.

q: What is foveal hypoplasia 1?
a: A genetic condition causing underdevelopment of the fovea, the part of the retina responsible for sharp central vision
d: A condition causing overgrowth of the fovea
Degeneration of the fovea in adults (age-related macular degeneration)
Inflammation of the fovea from infection (macular chorioretinitis)
Detachment of the retina starting at the fovea
A tumor arising from foveal cells
Foveal damage from excessive light exposure
Complete absence of the macula (macular aplasia)
Loss of foveal function from toxic drug exposure
e: Foveal hypoplasia 1 (FOVS1) is caused by mutations in the SLC38A8 gene. The fovea, the central pit of the retina, fails to fully develop during embryogenesis, resulting in reduced visual acuity, nystagmus (involuntary eye movement), and absent foveal avascular zone. It is associated with albinism-related conditions.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna. Distractors replaced with ophthalmology-specific alternatives.

q: What is FRA12A-type X-linked intellectual disability?
a: A form of X-linked intellectual disability associated with a fragile site on chromosome 12
d: A form of intellectual disability caused by trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)
A form of intellectual disability caused by fragile X syndrome (FMR1 mutation)
A form of intellectual disability caused by Turner syndrome (monosomy X)
A form of intellectual disability caused by a deletion on chromosome 15
A non-genetic intellectual disability caused by birth hypoxia
An acquired intellectual disability from childhood meningitis
A metabolic intellectual disability from phenylketonuria
A chromosomal inversion causing intellectual disability
e: FRA12A is a fragile site at chromosome 12q13.1. Intellectual disability associated with FRA12A is linked to mutations or methylation changes near the DIP2B gene in this region. Fragile sites are chromosomal loci susceptible to DNA breakage under conditions of replication stress.

q: What is chronic familial neutropenia?
a: A hereditary condition causing persistently low neutrophil counts, increasing susceptibility to bacterial infections
d: A temporary decrease in neutrophils caused by chemotherapy
A decrease in all white blood cells from bone marrow failure
Low neutrophils caused by autoimmune attack (autoimmune neutropenia)
Low neutrophils caused by viral infection
A condition of abnormally high neutrophil counts
Low neutrophils from severe nutritional deficiency
Low neutrophils from spleen over-activity (hypersplenism)
An acquired neutropenia from drug reaction
e: Chronic familial neutropenia is an autosomal dominant condition caused by mutations in genes such as ELA2 (neutrophil elastase). Affected individuals have a persistently low absolute neutrophil count, making them vulnerable to recurrent bacterial infections, oral ulcers, and periodontitis. It differs from cyclic neutropenia, where levels fluctuate.

q: What is premature chromatid separation trait?
a: A heritable tendency for sister chromatids to separate prematurely during cell division, predisposing to chromosomal instability
d: A disorder where chromosomes fail to separate at all (non-disjunction)
A condition where chromosomes break abnormally during mitosis
Normal variation in the timing of sister chromatid separation
A condition where chromosomes duplicate excessively
A disorder where spindle fibers cannot attach to chromosomes
A heritable tendency for chromosomal inversion
A form of chromosomal mosaicism without chromatid abnormalities
A telomere shortening disorder causing premature cell senescence
e: Premature chromatid separation (PCS) trait is a familial condition associated with mutations in genes encoding cohesin or its regulators. Premature separation of sister chromatids during meiosis leads to aneuploidy in eggs, increasing the risk of chromosomally abnormal pregnancies including Down syndrome.

q: What is progeria-short stature-pigmented nevi syndrome?
a: A rare progeroid (premature aging) syndrome characterized by short stature, multiple pigmented skin lesions, and lack of facial fat
d: Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome (classic progeria) with severe cardiovascular disease
Werner syndrome, an adult-onset progeroid condition
Cockayne syndrome, a progeroid condition with UV sensitivity
Rothmund-Thomson syndrome, a progeroid condition with skeletal dysplasia
A normal variant of early puberty with skin pigmentation
A nutritional syndrome causing short stature and skin changes
A hormonal disorder causing short stature and melanocytic changes
Down syndrome with premature aging features
e: Progeria-short stature-pigmented nevi syndrome is a rare autosomal recessive progeroid condition distinct from classic progeria (Hutchinson-Gilford). Key features include low birth weight, proportionate short stature, numerous pigmented nevi (moles), and absence of subcutaneous fat in the face, giving an aged appearance.

q: What is Wilms' tumor 2 (WT2)?
a: A genetic predisposition locus for Wilms' tumor (nephroblastoma), a childhood kidney cancer, distinct from the WT1 locus
d: The same genetic locus as Wilms' tumor 1 (WT1)
A Wilms' tumor variant occurring exclusively in adults
A mutation specifically in the WT1 gene causing bilateral kidney tumors
A syndrome causing both kidney and liver tumors
A Wilms' tumor subtype caused by a chromosomal deletion on 11p15
A sporadic Wilms' tumor with no genetic component
A kidney tumor associated with BRCA1 mutations
A cancer predisposition syndrome for adrenal tumors
e: Wilms' tumor 2 maps to chromosome 11p15.5 and involves imprinted genes in this region (IGF2/H19). Unlike WT1 (chromosome 11p13), the WT2 locus is associated with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome overgrowth syndrome, where imprinting defects at 11p15 predispose to Wilms' tumor formation.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna. Distractors replaced with Wilms' tumor genetics alternatives.

q: What is dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis?
a: A hereditary condition where red blood cells are dehydrated due to membrane cation leak, causing hemolytic anemia
d: Hereditary spherocytosis, where red cells are small and round from spectrin deficiency
Hereditary elliptocytosis, where red cells are oval-shaped
Sickle cell anemia, where red cells are sickle-shaped from hemoglobin mutation
Thalassemia, where globin chain production is reduced
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency causing episodic hemolysis
Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria causing complement-mediated red cell destruction
Iron deficiency anemia causing small pale red cells
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia from antibody attack on red cells
e: Dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis (DHSt) is caused by gain-of-function mutations in PIEZO1 (a mechanosensitive ion channel) or KCNN4. The cation leak dehydrates red blood cells, giving them a characteristic 'stomatocyte' (mouth-shaped) appearance and causing hemolytic anemia. Perinatal edema and pseudohyperkalemia are associated features.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna. Distractors replaced with hemolytic anemia type alternatives.

q: What is corticosterone methyloxidase type 1 deficiency?
a: A genetic deficiency of the enzyme that converts corticosterone to aldosterone, causing salt-wasting and electrolyte imbalance
d: A deficiency of cortisol (hydrocortisone) from adrenal cortex failure
A deficiency of adrenaline (epinephrine) from adrenal medulla failure
An excess of aldosterone causing high blood pressure (Conn's syndrome)
A deficiency of the enzyme that converts cholesterol to pregnenolone
An enzyme deficiency causing excess androgen production (congenital adrenal hyperplasia)
A parathyroid hormone deficiency causing low blood calcium
A thyroid hormone deficiency causing low metabolic rate
A deficiency of ACTH from pituitary failure
e: Corticosterone methyloxidase type 1 (CMO1) deficiency is caused by mutations in the CYP11B2 gene. CMO1 catalyzes the conversion of corticosterone to 18-hydroxycorticosterone, the step before aldosterone synthesis. Deficiency causes reduced aldosterone, salt-wasting, low blood pressure, hyperkalemia, and failure to thrive in infants.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna. Distractors replaced with adrenal/steroid enzyme deficiency alternatives.

q: What is the syndrome of cerebral malformation, seizures, hypertrichosis, and overlapping fingers?
a: A rare congenital syndrome combining brain structural abnormalities, seizures, excessive hair growth, and digital overlap
d: Angelman syndrome, causing seizures and developmental delay
Sturge-Weber syndrome, combining brain and skin vascular abnormalities
Trichothiodystrophy, combining hair abnormalities and neurological features
Coffin-Siris syndrome, combining intellectual disability and fifth finger hypoplasia
Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome, combining broad thumbs and intellectual disability
Wolf-Hirschhorn syndrome, a chromosome 4 deletion causing seizures
Cornelia de Lange syndrome, combining growth retardation and limb abnormalities
Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, a severe epilepsy without congenital malformations
e: This syndrome is a rare autosomal recessive condition caused by mutations in specific developmental genes. The combination of cortical malformations, early-onset seizures, hypertrichosis (excess hair), and overlapping or contractured fingers represents a distinctive clinical presentation used for molecular diagnosis.

q: What is combined Factor V and Factor VIII deficiency type 1?
a: A rare autosomal recessive bleeding disorder where both clotting Factor V and Factor VIII are deficient
d: Hemophilia A, caused by isolated Factor VIII deficiency
Hemophilia B, caused by isolated Factor IX deficiency
Von Willebrand disease, caused by deficiency of Von Willebrand factor
Factor V Leiden thrombophilia, an activated protein C resistance
Combined Factor VII and X deficiency
Vitamin K deficiency causing multiple factor deficiencies
Disseminated intravascular coagulation causing factor consumption
Platelet function disorder (thrombasthenia) without factor deficiency
e: Combined Factor V and VIII deficiency (F5F8D) type 1 is caused by mutations in LMAN1 (previously ERGIC-53), a lectin that acts as a chaperone for both Factor V and Factor VIII in the endoplasmic reticulum. Unlike hemophilia, both factors are simultaneously reduced, causing a mild to moderate bleeding tendency.

q: What is combined lipase deficiency?
a: A rare genetic disorder caused by LMF1 mutations that impairs the maturation of multiple lipases, causing hypertriglyceridemia
d: A deficiency of pancreatic lipase alone causing fat malabsorption
A deficiency of lipoprotein lipase alone causing hypertriglyceridemia
A deficiency of hepatic lipase alone affecting HDL metabolism
A dietary deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins caused by no fat digestion
An isolated fatty acid oxidation disorder
A glycogen storage disease affecting fat metabolism
An enzyme deficiency caused by chronic pancreatitis
A deficiency of bile salts needed to activate lipase
e: Combined lipase deficiency is caused by mutations in LMF1 (lipase maturation factor 1), which is required for the proper folding and secretion of multiple lipases including lipoprotein lipase (LPL) and hepatic lipase (HL). Without functional LMF1, both enzymes fail to mature, causing severely elevated triglycerides (chylomicronemia).
i: Improved question/answer. l2 changed from genetics_dna (already correct) to genetics_dna. Distractors replaced with lipase/lipid disorder alternatives.

q: What is myopathy with abnormal lipid metabolism?
a: A muscle disease caused by defective fatty acid oxidation or lipid storage in muscle cells
d: A myopathy caused by abnormal glycogen storage in muscle (glycogen storage myopathy)
A myopathy caused by mitochondrial DNA mutation without lipid abnormality
A myopathy caused by calcium channel dysfunction
A myopathy caused by sodium channel mutation (periodic paralysis)
An inflammatory myopathy (polymyositis) attacking muscle fibers
A myopathy caused by dystrophin protein deficiency (muscular dystrophy)
A myopathy caused by myosin heavy chain mutation
A myopathy caused by abnormal purine metabolism in muscle
e: Lipid storage myopathies involve defects in enzymes required for fatty acid oxidation or lipid transport in muscle, such as carnitine palmitoyltransferase II (CPT2) deficiency or multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (MADD). Excess lipid droplets accumulate within muscle fibers, causing weakness, exercise intolerance, and rhabdomyolysis.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to digestion_metabolism (metabolic muscle disease). Distractors replaced with myopathy type alternatives.

q: What is progesterone resistance?
a: A condition where target tissues fail to respond normally to progesterone, despite adequate hormone levels
d: A condition where progesterone levels are abnormally low (progesterone deficiency)
A condition where estrogen receptors are blocked by progesterone
Testosterone resistance (androgen insensitivity syndrome)
Cortisol resistance (glucocorticoid resistance syndrome)
Thyroid hormone resistance syndrome
A condition where the progesterone receptor is overexpressed
Insulin resistance causing hormonal imbalance
An autoimmune destruction of the corpus luteum reducing progesterone
e: Progesterone resistance occurs when the progesterone receptor (PGR) or its downstream signaling pathway is defective. It is associated with endometriosis, failed implantation, recurrent miscarriage, and failure of progesterone-based contraceptives. Mutations in the PGR gene or epigenetic silencing of progesterone response genes can cause resistance.

q: What is spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia with joint laxity type 1 (SEMDL)?

Multiple epiphyseal dysplasia without vertebral or metaphyseal involvement
Cleidocranial dysostosis affecting the skull and collarbone

e: SEMD with joint laxity type 1 (SEMDL1) is an autosomal recessive condition caused by mutations in B3GALT6 (encoding galactosyltransferase II), which affects proteoglycan synthesis. It presents with short-limbed short stature, platyspondyly (flattened vertebrae), metaphyseal widening, and severe joint hypermobility predisposing to fractures and dislocations.

q: What is immunodeficiency without anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia?
a: A form of NF-kB pathway immunodeficiency that causes recurrent infections without the skin and sweat gland abnormalities of ectodermal dysplasia
d: A combined T and B cell immunodeficiency (SCID)
An immunodeficiency with anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia with immunodeficiency, EDA-ID)
An immunodeficiency caused by complement protein deficiency
A phagocyte deficiency without lymphocyte abnormality
Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID)
An immunodeficiency caused by selective IgA deficiency
An autoimmune condition causing low immune cell counts
An immunodeficiency caused by thymocyte maturation failure
e: Immunodeficiency without anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (IDNEAD) is caused by mutations in NEMO (NF-kB essential modulator) that partially impair NF-kB signaling — severely enough to cause immune deficiency, but not so severely as to cause the full EDA-ID phenotype with skin and tooth abnormalities.

q: What is recurrent isolated invasive pneumococcal disease 2 (IPDI2)?
a: A genetic susceptibility to recurrent life-threatening Streptococcus pneumoniae infections without global immune deficiency
d: A form of pneumonia caused by Haemophilus influenzae, not pneumococcus
A form of pneumococcal disease in patients with no immune system at all (SCID)
A vaccine-preventable pneumococcal disease occurring once in unvaccinated individuals
An infection caused by penicillin-resistant pneumococcus only
A pneumococcal disease occurring only in elderly adults without genetic cause
An autoimmune lung disease triggered by S. pneumoniae antigens
A chronic lung disease increasing susceptibility to all respiratory infections
A form of COPD causing recurrent pneumococcal pneumonia
e: IPDI2 is caused by mutations in NEMO or other NF-kB pathway genes, or sometimes in TLR signaling genes, that selectively impair immunity to encapsulated bacteria like S. pneumoniae. Affected individuals have recurrent invasive episodes (bacteremia, meningitis) but normal responses to many other pathogens.

q: What is X-linked rolandic epilepsy with mental retardation and speech dyspraxia?
a: An X-linked genetic syndrome combining rolandic (centrotemporal) seizures, intellectual disability, and impaired speech motor control
d: A purely acquired epilepsy caused by birth hypoxia
Benign rolandic epilepsy of childhood (BREC), which resolves without intellectual disability

Fragile X syndrome, causing intellectual disability with autism features

ADHD with comorbid seizures without intellectual disability
e: X-linked rolandic epilepsy with mental retardation and speech dyspraxia (XMESID) is caused by mutations in the SRPX2 gene, which encodes a matrix protein involved in neuronal migration and speech area development. It affects both boys and girls, though boys are more severely affected.

q: What is X-linked atrophic macular degeneration?
a: A hereditary form of macular degeneration caused by an X-linked mutation, causing progressive central vision loss
d: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD), a non-genetic multifactorial disease
A form of macular degeneration caused by autosomal dominant mutation
A retinal dystrophy caused by mitochondrial DNA mutation
Stargardt disease, an autosomal recessive macular dystrophy from ABCA4 mutation
Best vitelliform macular dystrophy caused by BEST1 mutation
A macular degeneration caused by high myopia
Diabetic macular edema from long-term diabetes
Cone dystrophy causing central vision loss without photoreceptor atrophy
e: X-linked atrophic macular degeneration maps to chromosome Xq28 and involves progressive geographic atrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium and photoreceptors in the macular region. It differs from common age-related AMD in its younger onset, X-linked inheritance pattern, and distinct genetic etiology.

q: What is X-linked intellectual disability 101 (MRX101)?
a: A form of X-linked non-syndromic intellectual disability caused by mutation at the MRX101 genetic locus
d: Fragile X syndrome, the most common X-linked intellectual disability
An autosomal recessive intellectual disability
Down syndrome (trisomy 21) causing intellectual disability
Rett syndrome, an X-linked condition primarily affecting females

An intellectual disability caused by congenital hypothyroidism
A syndromic X-linked intellectual disability with additional physical features
Angelman syndrome caused by loss of UBE3A function

q: What is MRXS99F (X-linked syndromic intellectual disability 99, female-restricted)?
a: A form of X-linked syndromic intellectual disability that predominantly or exclusively affects females rather than males
d: A form of X-linked intellectual disability affecting only males
An autosomal intellectual disability with female sex bias
Rett syndrome, the most well-known X-linked condition affecting primarily females
Turner syndrome (45,X), a chromosomal condition causing learning difficulties
Fragile X syndrome in female carriers
A mitochondrial disease causing intellectual disability preferentially in females

In MRXS99F and similar conditions, the mutation causes a condition in which hemizygous males may die prenatally (non-viable), so only heterozygous females are observed clinically. This is an X-linked dominant, male-lethal pattern.

q: What is Bain type X-linked syndromic intellectual disability?

q: What is CILD36 (X-linked primary ciliary dyskinesia 36)?
a: An X-linked genetic disorder causing immotile or dysfunctional cilia, leading to chronic respiratory infections and male infertility
d: Cystic fibrosis, an autosomal recessive disease causing thick mucus in the lungs

e: Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) is usually autosomal recessive, but CILD36 is an X-linked form. Cilia throughout the body are abnormal, leading to chronic airway infections (bronchitis, sinusitis), bronchiectasis, and male infertility from immotile sperm. The X-linked form involves mutations in genes on the X chromosome encoding ciliary structural proteins.

q: What is primary autosomal recessive microcephaly 2 (MCPH2)?
a: A genetic form of primary microcephaly caused by an autosomal recessive mutation, resulting in a small but structurally normal brain
d: A form of microcephaly caused by intrauterine Zika virus infection

e: Primary microcephaly type 2 (MCPH2) is caused by mutations in WDR62, which encodes a centrosomal protein essential for mitotic spindle assembly. Defects in centrosome function impair symmetric neural progenitor division, reducing the total number of cortical neurons and resulting in a small but architecturally normal brain.

q: What is melanoma-pancreatic cancer syndrome?

e: Familial melanoma-pancreatic cancer syndrome is caused by germline mutations in CDKN2A, which encodes p16 (INK4a), a cell cycle regulator and tumor suppressor. Individuals with CDKN2A mutations have significantly elevated lifetime risk of melanoma (up to 60-90%) and pancreatic cancer (up to 17%), compared to the general population.
